subject: Accounting
chapter_name: Irrecoverable debts and provisions for doubtful debts

note_data.title: Irrecoverable debts and provisions for doubtful debts
keyPoints: Irrecoverable debts are debts that cannot be recovered from customers and must be written off from the accounts
Double entries are required when dealing with irrecoverable debts to properly record the loss in the accounting system
Provision of debts is created to account for potential future losses from customers who may not pay their outstanding amounts
The process involves identifying debts that are unlikely to be collected and making appropriate accounting adjustments
Irrecoverable debts directly impact the profit and loss account as they represent actual losses to the business
Provision for doubtful debts is an estimate of potential losses and helps in presenting a more accurate financial position
Both irrecoverable debts and provisions for doubtful debts affect the value of accounts receivable shown in the balance sheet

definitions.definition: Debts that cannot be recovered from customers and must be written off from the accounts

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all workings clearly and label your final answer
Use proper account format with correct titles and column headings

modelAnswer.question: Ahmed's business has debtors of Rs.50,000 at 31 December 2023. During the year, irrecoverable debts of Rs.2,000 were written off. A provision for doubtful debts is to be maintained at 5% of debtors. The provision brought forward was Rs.2,600. Calculate the charge/credit to profit and loss account.
modelAnswer.answer:
Provision required: Rs.50,000 × 5% = Rs.2,500
Provision brought forward: Rs.2,600
Decrease in provision: Rs.2,600 - Rs.2,500 = Rs.100
Charge to P&L account:
Irrecoverable debts written off: Rs.2,000 (expense)
Decrease in provision: Rs.100 (income)
Net charge to P&L: Rs.2,000 - Rs.100 = Rs.1,900
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Calculation of provision required Rs.2,500
Mark 2: Identification of decrease in provision Rs.100
Mark 3: Irrecoverable debts Rs.2,000 as expense
Mark 4: Net charge Rs.1,900 to P&L account

commonMistakes: Confusing irrecoverable debts with provision for doubtful debts
Incorrectly recording the double entries for debt write-offs
Not distinguishing between actual losses and estimated potential losses
examinerTips: Ensure correct double entries are made when writing off irrecoverable debts
Understand the difference between irrecoverable debts and provision of debts
formulas: Change in provision = New provision - Old provision
Net debtors = Gross debtors - Provision for doubtful debts
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account format showing Provision for Doubtful Debts account with opening balance, increase/decrease, and closing balance, clearly linking to P&L account entries
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate the provision for doubtful debts and show journal entries for writing off a specific debt
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (4 marks) — required students to identify the correct treatment of irrecoverable debts in the income statement and statement of financial position
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (8 marks) — comprehensive question asking for calculation of new provision, adjustment entries, and effect on profit for the year
N/D 2021 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — tested recovery of previously written-off debt and the correct accounting treatment in ledger accounts

workedExample.question: ABC Ltd has trade receivables of $45,000 at year-end. The provision for doubtful debts should be 4% of receivables. The existing provision is $1,500. During the year, debts of $800 were written off as irrecoverable. Calculate the adjustment needed and show the income statement extract. (6 marks)
steps: Step 2: Compare with existing provision = $1,800 - $1,500 = $300 increase needed
Step 3: Journal entry: Dr Irrecoverable debts expense $300, Cr Provision for doubtful debts $300
Step 4: Income statement shows: Irrecoverable debts written off $800 + Increase in provision $300 = $1,100 total expense
workedExample.answer: Adjustment required: Increase provision by $300. Income Statement extract: Irrecoverable debts expense $1,100 (comprising $800 debts written off and $300 increase in provision).
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct calculation of required provision, 2 marks for identifying adjustment needed, 2 marks for correct income statement presentation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused irrecoverable debts with bad debt provisions' — always distinguish between actual write-offs (irrecoverable debts) and estimated future losses (provisions)
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students incorrectly showed provisions as assets instead of contra-assets' — always remember provisions reduce the receivables figure in the statement of financial position

Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was adding the full provision amount to expenses instead of only the movement' — only the increase or decrease in provision affects the income statement, not the total provision balance
note_data.keyDiagram: T-account layout showing: (1) Trade Receivables account with opening balance, credit sales, and deductions for irrecoverable debts, (2) Provision for Doubtful Debts account showing opening balance, any decreases/increases, and closing balance, (3) Irrecoverable Debts Expense account showing debts written off plus movement in provision. Include arrows showing the flow from receivables to provision to expense accounts.
note_data.quickRecap: Irrecoverable debts are amounts that cannot be collected from customers and must be written off using proper double entries. Provision of debts is created to estimate potential future losses. Both concepts are essential for accurate financial reporting and affect the accounts receivable balance.
